Article Title: Explained: OpenAI’s GPT‑5.6 series' Sol, Terra and Luna AI models
Article Text:
OpenAI (logo)

OpenAI GPT 5.6 limited rollout



Good news first: Sol is a smart, efficient, and a significant step forward. It is the same price as GPT-5.5. Also launching in the GPT-5.6 family is Terra, with 5.5-level performance at half the price.

Bad news: at the request of the US government, it is launching today in limited preview instead of the open access launch we were planning on. We are working with the government to get to general availability as fast as we can.

I think it is quite reasonable to roll out models--especially as they reach significant new levels of capability--in this way. It fits with our long-held strategy of iterative deployment. But this isn't quite the process that we think is optimal.

Now we will with the government to attempt to get to a transparent, reliable process for early access, and to ensure that as long as our safeguards work as intended we can release widely. We want to be a reliable, dependable partner that works with all stakeholders, and we also want to live by our mission of benefiting all of humanity. I believe the government shares most of our goals, and that they are overall doing a good job in a very difficult situation.

We will work as quickly as we can to get this model in your hands and we hope you will love it.

Understanding the lineup: Sol, Terra and Luna

GPT-5.6 Sol is the elite flagship model with maximum reasoning, deep coding and cyber defense

GPT-5.6 Terra is the balanced tier, and it can handle everyday enterprise workloads at 2x cost savings

GPT-5.6 Luna is the fastest of the lot and it is OpenAI’s lowest-cost option for basic tasks

OpenAI GPT 5.6 Sol battles Anthropic’s Mythos



Refusal training:

Real-time classifiers:

Account monitoring:

Availability

OpenAI recently unveiled the GPT-5.6 series of artificial intelligence models even as the company moves closer to counter intense competitive pressure and stringent regulatory scrutiny. The AI company introduced three distinct capability tiers designed to balance raw computing power with affordability: the flagship Sol, the mid-tier Terra and the hyper-fast Luna.Rather than executing a wide public rollout, OpenAI is launching the series in a tightly restricted “limited preview.” The targeted launch comes at the request of the US government, following high-profile national security scrutiny where the Trump administration utilised export controls to render rival models offline but later allowed them with some conditions.OpenAI noted it is working alongside Washington ahead of an upcoming White House cyber Executive Order, though the tech giant warned that heavy government intervention should not become the industry’s permanent default.OpenAI CEO Sam Altman said:OpenAI’s new naming convention separates the models (GPT-5.6) from its durable performance tiers. The family is priced per 1 million tokens, providing a clear spectrum of operational choices.To push capabilities further, OpenAI is debuting two new processing modes for its premium Sol model. A “max reasoning effort” mode allocates extra computing time for the AI to think deeply. Furthermore, a new “ultra mode” allows Sol to break away from the limits of a single agent by autonomously deploying and managing multiple “subagents” to accelerate massive, multi-step projects.The most profound advancements in the GPT-5.6 series reside in cybersecurity. OpenAI claims Sol completely shifts the performance-efficiency frontier for vulnerability research. On ExploitBench, Sol managed to tie the performance of Anthropic’s restricted, highly controversial Mythos Preview model. Sol achieved this while utilising only one-third of the output tokens required by its Anthropic rival, representing a massive leap forward in processing efficiency.Additionally, on ExploitGym – a collaborative security benchmark built by UC Berkeley researchers – all three models (Sol, Terra, and Luna) demonstrated linear improvements in hacking and defense capabilities as their reasoning parameters were increased.As these models display advanced autonomous capabilities, OpenAI built them with its most rigid safeguard stack to date. The company stressed that Sol is intentionally engineered to be better at finding and patching code bugs than successfully executing end-to-end cyberattacks.To maintain this boundary, OpenAI is deploying a layered safety stack:The model is trained to spot disguised intent and automatically refuse requests for prohibited cyber assistance, OpenAI says.Algorithms actively scan outputs as they generate. If a violation is suspected, the text pauses mid-sentence while a secondary reasoning model reviews the context before deciding to block it.Thirdly, OpenAI will track risk signals across entire user histories, helping systems distinguish malicious actors from legitimate security researchers performing dual-use defensive testing.To ensure these defenses hold up against evolving threats, OpenAI is said to have burned over 700,000 A100-equivalent GPU hours on automated “red-teaming”.During the current preview phase, access to the GPT-5.6 family remains restricted to select API developers, Codex users and government-vetted partners. OpenAI plans to expand availability to ChatGPT Plus users and broader enterprise clients in the coming weeks.
Article Summary: OpenAI's GPT-5.6 series of AI models launched in limited preview at the request of the US government. The company says it is working with Washington ahead of an upcoming White House cyber Executive Order. The tech giant warned that heavy government intervention should not become the industry’s permanent default.

Article Title: AI helps read 2,000-year-old papyrus scroll PHerc 1667 as scientists fully unwrap it, revealing…
Article Text:
How a charred scroll finally gave up its secrets

What the ancient text actually says

hormē

phronēsis

Poll Vote & Share your view Would you consider entering a contest to read an unopened scroll? Definitely! No, it's too difficult. 3k+ users shared opinion today 5k+ users already voted today 3k+ users shared opinion today Share Opinion

A scroll so badly charred by the eruption of Mount Vesuvius that scholars once gave it a readability score of zero has now been read—virtually unwrapped end to end, without a single layer being touched. Researchers from the University of Kentucky and Naples announced the breakthrough Thursday at the Biblioteca Nazionale in Naples, with the volcano that buried it nearly 2,000 years ago visible in the distance. The scroll, named PHerc. 1667, gave up nearly 1.5 metres of continuous Greek text across 20 columns, all of it surfaced with the help of artificial intelligence.PHerc. 1667 is among the oldest in the Herculaneum collection, dated to the second or possibly late third century BC. It survives as only half its original self—about 8cm tall and 2cm wide—after centuries of failed attempts to physically pry it open flaked away its outer layers. Records from 1782 describe it as compressed but largely intact; the damage came later, from people trying to read it. When part of it was opened in the 1980s, it was declared unreadable.The work comes out of the Vesuvius Challenge, a global contest Brent Seales launched in 2023 with Silicon Valley backers Nat Friedman and Daniel Gross, building on research he had been chipping away at since 2002.The method starts with high-resolution micro-CT scans, done at Diamond Light Source in the UK and the ESRF in France. Some scans ran as large as 300 terabytes per scroll—the biggest dataset ESRF has ever produced. AI trained to spot ink then reads the flattened layers."This scroll was deemed completely unreadable when part of it was opened in the 1980s," said papyrologist Federica Nicolardi. "But now, with virtual unwrapping, we can follow sustained arguments across multiple columns. That's a transformational shift."The contents read as a Stoic treatise on ethics, art and human behaviour—unusual, since the Herculaneum library is almost entirely Epicurean. Nicolardi's team suspects Chrysippus, the third head of the Stoic school, partly because the text names his nephew and pupil Aristocreon. It discusses, or impulse, warning against unchecked behaviour, and, practical wisdom, the highest Stoic virtue. One line reads: "We will inquire into something, but we will not grasp it, if in some way we depart from ourselves and from our own nature."A second scroll, PHerc. 139, revealed the words "Philodemus, On Gods, Book 8," proving for the first time that the work ran to at least eight books rather than one. Researchers also pulled 70-plus columns from PHerc. 172, identified as "On Vices, Book 1."More than 600 scrolls remain unopened. The University of Kentucky has now offered a fresh $1 million prize to anyone who can fully read another by next June.
Article Summary: The scroll is among the oldest in the Herculaneum collection, dated to the second or possibly late third century BC. It survives as only half its original self, after centuries of failed attempts to physically pry it open. The contents read as a Stoic treatise on ethics, art and human behaviour.

Article Title: Chinese and Japanese companies launch AI models, claim they are as powerful as Anthropic’s Mythos
Article Text:
Japan’s Sakana AI rolls out ‘Fugu’



China’s 360 launches Cyber AI model and tool



$47 billion market in tough spot



Two AI companies in Japan and China have launched AI models for cybersecurity and claim that their new software matches the power of Anthropic's restricted “Mythos” and “Fable 5” models even as the US government maintains its strict export ban on Anthropic's most advanced piece of technology. The sudden regional product launches come just two weeks after the Trump administration deployed emergency national security export controls, abruptly pulling Anthropic’s cutting-edge systems offline for all non-American users.In Tokyo, prominent AI startup Sakana AI launched a new frontier model called “Fugu” – named after the Japanese word for blowfish. The company claims that Fugu “stands shoulder-to-shoulder” with Anthropic’s banned models, but with one critical geopolitical advantage: it delivers top-tier capabilities entirely free from the risk of American export controls.Co-founded in 2023 by former Google researchers Ren Ito, Llion Jones and David Ha, Sakana specialises in building efficient generative AI models tailored for Asian languages and cultures, according to a report by TechCrunch .Fugu is engineered to act as an “orchestration model” for autonomous AI agents. Rather than just answering prompts, Fugu can automatically coordinate and manage tasks across multiple other AI models using their APIs.“Access to top models can disappear overnight. Relying on a single provider for national infrastructure is a risk... Collective intelligence is the practical hedge against this concentration of power,” Sakana CEO David Ha wrote on X. A spokesperson for Sakana told TechCrunch that the timing of the launch was “entirely coincidental” based on research planned since last year.While Japan's Sakana is framing its model as a defensive backup plan for US allies, Chinese cybersecurity giant 360 took a much more aggressive stance. The Beijing-based firm unveiled two powerful new AI security systems: one is Tulongfeng an AI model engineered to go head-to-head with Anthropic's Mythos by automatically scanning and discovering hidden software vulnerabilities.And there is Yitianzhen, which is a tool built to automate rapid cyber defense and real-time incident response. According to news agency Reuters, 360 founder Zhou Hongyi described vulnerability-detecting AI as a “national strategic asset.”The rapid emergence of local alternatives may dent American dominance in the region. Prior to the government intervention, Anthropic was on a historic financial trajectory, reporting that its run-rate revenue crossed a staggering $47 billion in May 2026.
Article Summary: Japan's Sakana AI and China's 360 launch new AI models for cybersecurity. Sakana claims its 'Fugu' model matches the power of Anthropic's restricted 'Mythos' and 'Fable 5' models. But Fugu is entirely free from the risk of American export controls.

Article Title: Quote of the day by Elon Musk: "Just believe your interaction. The resume may seem very impressive…but…" - why he trusts people over paper
Article Text:
Quote of the day by Elon Musk

Quote of the day by Elon Musk

"Just believe your interaction. The resume may seem very impressive…but if the conversation after 20 minutes is not 'Wow,' you should believe the conversation, not the paper."

Why Elon Musk stopped trusting resumes

What is the meaning of Elon Musk’s quote

Why this quote by Elon Musk is relevant

How to apply Elon Musk’s quote in daily life

Weight direct experience over labels. Whether it's a hire, a contractor, a flatmate or a date, give more weight to how the real interaction feels than to the tidy summary.

Look for a genuine 'wow.' Not politeness, not a neat rehearsed answer, but a moment where someone actually impresses you. If twenty minutes pass without it, take note.

Don't be hypnotised by pedigree. A famous school or employer is a clue, not a guarantee. Plenty of brilliant people have plain résumés, and the reverse is just as true.

Cross-check your gut. A first impression can be wrong, so where it matters, look for a second conversation or a concrete example before you fully commit.

Other famous quotes by Elon Musk

"When something is important enough, you do it even if the odds are not in your favor."

"Failure is an option here. If things are not failing, you are not innovating enough."

"Persistence is very important. You should not give up unless you are forced to give up."

"I think it's possible for ordinary people to choose to be extraordinary."

Most of us have been trained to worship the résumé. The right schools, the big-name employers, the neat list of titles marching down the page. Elon Musk says that's exactly the wrong thing to trust. Just believe your interaction, he advises. The résumé may seem very impressive, but if the conversation after 20 minutes is not 'Wow,' you should believe the conversation, not the paper. It's blunt hiring advice from a man who has hired a great many people. But it carries a wider idea too. A piece of paper is a polished summary, written to impress. A real conversation is the actual person, thinking out loud in front of you. When the two disagree, Musk's rule is simple. Trust what you saw with your own eyes, not what was typed up to sell you.Musk shared the line in early 2026, on a podcast with Stripe cofounder John Collison and Dwarkesh Patel. He was honest about why he'd changed his mind. He admitted falling for what he called the pixie dust effect, the assumption that someone from a famous company like Google or Apple must automatically be brilliant. Often, he found, they weren't.So now he looks for something else. He wants evidence of exceptional ability, real proof that a person has actually done something hard, rather than just a list of places they've worked.He also said he learned, a little late, to value goodness of heart and trustworthiness, and not only raw talent. The conversation, not the credentials, is where he tries to see all of that.At its core the quote is about trusting direct evidence over a stand-in. A résumé is secondhand. It's a carefully edited advertisement for a person, often shaped by someone who knows how to make ordinary work sound extraordinary. A conversation is firsthand. You get to watch how someone actually thinks, reacts and explains themselves under a bit of pressure.Musk's point is that when those two sources clash, the firsthand one should win. A dazzling CV that produces a flat, forgettable conversation is a warning, not a reassurance. And a modest CV attached to a person who genuinely makes you sit up and think 'wow' is worth far more than the paper lets on. Believe the live version, he's saying, over the press release.This goes well beyond hiring. We're surrounded by impressive-looking paper. Polished profiles, glowing reviews, long lists of credentials, big follower counts. It's easy to be talked into respecting something just because it looks good on the page, even when our own experience of it falls flat.The quote is a nudge to trust that experience. If a product has rave reviews but feels cheap in your hands, believe your hands. If someone has a glittering reputation but every actual interaction leaves you cold, pay attention to that. None of this means first impressions are perfect, since a single chat can mislead too. But the deeper lesson holds. Don't let an impressive label quietly overrule what's right in front of you.You don't need to be running a company to use this.Strip away the billionaire and the headlines, and the advice is something most of us already half know but rarely follow. Paper can be dressed up. A real conversation is much harder to fake. So the next time the résumé and the person in front of you tell two different stories, remember Musk's rule. Believe the person.
Article Summary: Musk shared the line in early 2026, on a podcast with Stripe cofounder John Collison and Dwarkesh Patel. He admitted falling for what he called the pixie dust effect, the assumption that someone from a famous company like Google or Apple must automatically be brilliant.

Article Title: Vodafone Idea postpaid users get complimentary Spotify Premium subscription
Article Text:
Benefits for Vi postpaid customers



Ad-Free Music: Uninterrupted access to over 100 million tracks and 7 million podcasts.

Offline Downloads: Save playlists, albums, and podcasts for listening anywhere.

Unlimited Control: Play any track, skip freely, and manage queues across devices.

High-Quality Audio: Premium streaming quality up to 320 kbps.

Exclusive Free Back Cover: Vi remains the only telco offering bundled lifestyle benefits with connectivity.

Vodafone Idea also known as Vihas announced a strategic partnership with Spotify to deliver premium music streaming experiences to its postpaid customers. Starting today, Vi Postpaid subscribers on eligible plans will receive three months of complimentary Spotify Premium access at no additional cost. The collaboration combines Vi’s robust connectivity with Spotify’s world-class audio platform, offering ad-free listening, offline downloads, unlimited skips, and high-quality streaming at up to 320 kbps.Eligible users who have not previously subscribed to Spotify can activate their complimentary Premium subscription via the Vi App.Avneesh Khosla, Chief Marketing Officer at Vi, said: “Music is central to our customers’ digital lives. Partnering with Spotify strengthens our entertainment ecosystem and offers unmatched content experiences that make Vi the top choice for subscribers.”Amarjit Batra, Managing Director of Spotify India, added: “Through our first-of-its-kind telco partnership in India with Vi, we are making Spotify Premium more accessible to millions of music and podcast fans. This is about setting a new standard for high-quality listening and unlimited control.”The Spotify Premium benefit is available across Vi’s Individual Postpaid plans priced at Rs 451, Rs 551, Rs 751, and Rs 1201 (REDX), as well as Family Postpaid plans ranging from Rs 701 to Rs 1601. For family plans, the benefit applies to the primary member.After the three-month complimentary period, subscribers can continue Spotify Premium via Vi’s ‘Add to Bill’ facility at Rs 139 per month.
Article Summary: Vodafone Idea also known as Vihas announced a strategic partnership with Spotify to deliver premium music streaming experiences to its postpaid customers. Starting today, Vi Postpaid subscribers on eligible plans will receive three months of complimentary Spotify Premium access at no additional cost.

Article Title: After clearing Anthropic’s Mythos 5 for a trusted few, the US government may free Fable 5 next
Article Text:
Fable 5 is almost back. Just the Pentagon and NSA left to say yes.

Why developers cared so much about losing Fable

What still stands between Fable and its comeback

Anthropic's most powerful public AI model, Fable 5, may be back online as soon as next week, according to Axios, marking a possible end to a blackout that left developers stranded mid-task and scrambling for replacements. The model has been dark for 15 days, ever since the US government ordered Anthropic to pull it over cybersecurity fears. Two sources told Axios that talks were expected to continue through the weekend, with the administration's limits potentially lifting in the coming days.The signs of a thaw are already showing. On Friday, the Commerce Department let Anthropic restore access to Mythos 5, its strongest cybersecurity model, for a small set of trusted users—cyber defenders and infrastructure providers who operate critical systems. Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick wrote to Anthropic that the company "has worked with the US government to address risks" tied to both models, and that those efforts had "yielded significant progress. " Mythos 5, unlike Fable, has never been freely available and ships with guardrails meant to deter cyberattacks and biological misuse.Fable 5's disappearance hit hard because the model had earned real devotion fast.Anthropic billed it as the most capable model ever released to the public, and early users agreed. Every's "Vibe Check" newsletter called it the best coding model in the world before it vanished—three days after launch. In testing Anthropic highlighted, payments firm Stripe used Fable 5 to rework a 50-million-line codebase in a single day, a job its engineers said would have taken more than two months by hand.When access cut out on June 12, automated workflows froze and companies rushed to swap in rivals, including cheaper Chinese models. For non-technical early adopters, a top-tier tool being yanked from their hands felt unprecedented.The path back isn't fully clear. The Pentagon and National Security Agency still need to sign off before Fable 5 can return, so the outcome stays unpredictable. Other agencies, the source said, have already decided the model can safely go back into the wild. Behind the scenes, Lutnick and Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent reportedly helped cool a four-month feud—a notable shift from Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth's earlier move labeling Anthropic a "Supply-Chain Risk to National Security. "Two open questions remain for users. It's unclear whether subscribers will get back the free Fable access they were promised through June 22, or whether it returns behind extra fees or identity checks. Both Anthropic and OpenAI, meanwhile, are pushing the administration to replace its case-by-case reviews with a formal, repeatable process for vetting new models—something both companies argue the current standoff has shown is badly needed.
Article Summary: Anthropic's most powerful public AI model, Fable 5, may be back online as soon as next week. The model has been dark for 15 days, ever since the US government ordered Anthropic to pull it over cybersecurity fears. Two sources told Axios that talks were expected to continue through the weekend, with the administration's limits potentially lifting.

Article Title: Oracle layoffs: Over 500 employees get Oracle Leadership’s 6am email; saying: 'You are a..'
Article Text:
Oracle has begun a second round of layoffs in Romania, notifying roughly 500 employees on June 25 as its AI-driven restructuring rolls on.

Oracle's second round of Romania cuts was planned months ago

The numbers behind the building in Bucharest

Oracle's layoffs have reached Romania again. Starting June 25, the company began notifying roughly 500 employees there that their roles were on the chopping block, with affected staff describing emails headed "COLLECTIVE DISMISSAL - YOU ARE A PROPOSED IMPACTED EMPLOYEE. " Oracle hasn't disclosed the scale publicly, but the round follows a pattern its staff elsewhere know well: in earlier US and India cuts, employees described emails from "Oracle Leadership" hitting their inboxes as early as 6AM, with no HR call and no manager warning. For one of Oracle's largest engineering hubs in central and eastern Europe, June 25 marked the day the second axe fell.The tally sits at around 520, according to posts on the r/employeesOfOracle subreddit, where affected staff traded details within hours of the first emails. That lands on a Romanian operation of roughly 4,000 people, and it is the second such sweep there in under a year. The pace is what rattled even those left standing, with whole teams reportedly wiped in a single morning.This was not a snap decision. A former Oracle employee, speaking to Ziarul Financiar, said the latest cuts were part of a restructuring mapped out in the previous fiscal year, with the list of names drawn up as far back as spring.The company had already let go of about 400 Romanian staff in late 2025—then the largest restructuring in its history—so the June round lands on an operation still raw from the first.The cut reaches across the business rather than one corner of it. The lines of business flagged as potentially impacted span Oracle Cloud Infrastructure (OCI), Database Technologies, One Oracle EMEA, Industries Applications, Oracle Health & Analytics, Customer Success Services, Fusion Applications Development & Support, General & Administrative, and Other Technology Groups—a spread that takes in core engineering, support, and back-office roles alike.The mechanics, as described by employees online, were clinical. The emails went out individually rather than as one mass notice, sent from HR with managers copied in and documents attached. The "proposed" wording left some unsure whether the decision was final, with one poster noting affected staff could try to "negotiate" their case. Because the notices were staggered, employees who hadn't heard by midday weren't necessarily safe. Slack and internal accounts, several noted, were still active even for those already told.Severance, by workers' accounts, runs to roughly one month's pay per year worked, plus around three compensatory salaries and two months of garden leave—broadly in line with the earlier Romanian round. As one employee put it, the cuts weren't performance-driven but the cost of the company's AI bet. The dread is what lingers: the former employee who spoke to Ziarul Financiar warned more adjustments could follow.Oracle's three main Romanian entities employed 4,288 people at the end of 2025, according to finance ministry filings. Revenue there actually grew—combined sales of about 1.79 billion lei in 2025, up 7.5% on the year. Yet the same entities slipped into a combined net loss of 34.8 million lei after a small profit in 2024. Rising revenue, shrinking headcount: the contradiction sits at the heart of why these jobs went.Romania built much of its tech economy on exactly this kind of work—the engineering and support functions multinationals park where skilled labour is cheaper. Those are the functions an AI-driven reorganisation reaches first, which is why the country's large tech-services sector is so exposed to decisions made in California.The Romanian cuts are a small tributary of a much larger river. Oracle's global headcount fell to about 141,000 by the end of May 2026, down from 162,000 a year earlier—a drop of nearly 13%, funded by a data centre build-out the company is partly paying for by cutting payroll. For the people in Bucharest, though, the cut was not a line in a filing. It was a notice with their name on it—and on the former employee's account, spring's list may not be the last.
Article Summary: Oracle has begun a second round of layoffs in Romania, notifying roughly 500 employees on June 25. The tally sits at around 520, according to posts on the r/employeesOfOracle subreddit. That lands on a Romanian operation of roughly 4,000 people, and it is the second such sweep there in under a year.

Article Title: Jeff Bezos' ex-wife MacKenzie Scott who alone made one-third of America's billions of mega donations in 2025 is missing from 'Chronicle of Philanthropy' list as it is 'impossible to ...'
Article Text:
Why MacKenzie Scott was missing from the list

Net worth still strong

MacKenzie Scott donated $26 billion



Scott believes in trust-based philanthropy



Amazon founder Jeff Bezos’ ex-wife and billionaire philanthropist MacKenzie Scott donated around $7 billion in 2025, accounting for more than one-third of America’s largest charitable gifts that year, according to a report by Fortune. The contributions made by Scott helped power a record $617.2 billion in overall U.S. charitable giving, according to Giving USA and the Indiana University Lilly Family School of Philanthropy. The other major donors included Michael Bloomberg ($4.3 billion), Bill Gates ($3.7 billion), Warren Buffett ($1.34 billion), and Susan and Michael Dell (nearly $1 billion). Despite making all these donations Scott failed to appear in the Chronicle of Philanthropy’s annual list.Despite the massive donations made by Scott, she did not appear on the annual philanthropy ranking list of Chronicle of Philanthropy. The publication mentioned that it was ‘impossible’ to verify all the contributions made by Scott as her representatives decline to provide all the needed information. Also, Scott has consistently avoided publicity around her donations and has always preferred a low-profile approach.Since the year 2020, Scott has donated around $26.2 billion to thousands of organisations supporting affordable housing, disaster recovery, diversity initiatives, and education. In a December essay, Scott reflected: “It is these ripple effects that make imagining the power of any of our own acts of kindness impossible.”Even after her massive donations, Scott’s net worth remains around $30 billion, largely due to the appreciation of her Amazon holdings. Amazon shares have gained more than 33% over the past five years, ensuring her continued presence among the world’s wealthiest individuals.According to a report by Fortune, Scott has given away more than $26 billion across 2,700 gifts through her philanthropic organization, Yield Giving. The year 2025 was the biggest year of her donations. Last Scott donated $7.2 billion which is more than both Jeff Bezos and Lauren Sánchez Bezos have given in their lifetimes, as per Forbes. This year, Scott also received the title of the third-most generous philanthropist globally, having already given away 46% of her net worth.As per the Fortune report, Scott’s style of giving is unconventional. She writes unrestricted checks with no applications, progress reports, or publicity requirements. Philanthropy experts describe her approach as trust-based giving, empowering institutions to use funds freely. “She practices trust-based philanthropy,” said Anne Marie Dougherty, CEO of the Bob Woodruff Foundation.
Article Summary: MacKenzie Scott donated $7 billion in 2025, accounting for more than one-third of America’s largest charitable gifts. The contributions made by Scott helped power a record $617.2 billion in overall U.S. charitable giving, according to Giving USA and the Indiana University Lilly Family School of Philanthropy. This year, Scott received the title of the third-most generous philanthropist globally, having already given away 46% of her net worth.

Article Title: WhatsApp gets a new chief from India, and India gets a bigger role at Meta
Article Text:
As part of the reported transition, Kunal Shah is expected to step down from his role as Chief Executive Officer of CRED and join Meta's global leadership team.

You have to wonder whether Mark Zuckerberg or Meta’s chief product officer Chris Cox first sent Kunal Shah a “Good morning” message on WhatsApp before asking him to run the app. Either way, the Cred founder’s elevation to the top job marks a rare moment when one of India’s best-known startup entrepreneurs takes charge of a product used by more than three billion people.Kunal Shah has spent much of his career building businesses around consumer behaviour and trust. Now, he inherits a product that already sits at the centre of everyday life for billions of people.Meta’s decision to hand the reins of WhatsApp to the Cred founder is more than a high-profile executive appointment. It signals where the company sees its next growth engine and underlines the increasing importance of India to Meta’s ambitions, both as a market and as a testing ground for new products.Shah takes over at a pivotal moment for WhatsApp. Nearly 12 years after Meta paid $19 billion for the messaging app, the company is still figuring out how to turn its scale into a business that goes beyond advertising. With over three billion monthly users globally and India accounting for its biggest audience, WhatsApp has become too important to remain merely a utility.Industry estimates peg WhatsApp’s user base in India at more than 600 million.In many ways, the app has become a proxy for internet penetration in the country. From neighbourhood stores and doctors to schools and government agencies, WhatsApp has evolved into the default layer through which Indians communicate, transact and increasingly consume services.That ubiquity has made India central to Meta’s monetisation plans. Business messaging, which allows companies to communicate with customers over WhatsApp, has emerged as one of Meta’s fastest-growing businesses. The company said earlier this year that paid messaging had crossed a $2 billion annualised revenue run rate. India, one would believe, contributes a sizeable portion of that growth, being the biggest market for WhatsApp.The company has also used India as a laboratory for several WhatsApp products, ranging from business tools and AI agents to citizen-service initiatives with state governments. Andhra Pradesh, Maharashtra, Odisha and Tamil Nadu have all experimented with WhatsApp-based public service delivery systems.Shah's appointment comes as Meta attempts to build entirely new revenue streams around the messaging platform. Advertising in WhatsApp’s Updates tab, Channels, subscriptions and commerce are all still in their early stages. Payments remain another unfinished piece of the puzzle.Despite years of effort, WhatsApp Pay continues to lag rivals such as PhonePe and Google Pay. While the platform has crossed 150 million monthly UPI transactions, its market share remains below one percent. Meta has increasingly shifted towards positioning WhatsApp as a broader commerce layer where transactions can happen through UPI, cards and net banking rather than relying solely on payments market share.The appointment also carries strategic significance beyond products and revenue.Meta has found itself under growing regulatory and legal scrutiny in India. The company continues to battle concerns over privacy, data sharing and competition. The Supreme Court has repeatedly questioned WhatsApp and Meta over data practices, warning that citizens' privacy cannot be compromised for commercial interests. Separately, the Competition Commission of India has scrutinised WhatsApp's privacy policy changes and Meta's dominance in digital markets.Having one of India's best-known entrepreneurs at the helm of WhatsApp could help Meta navigate that environment better. Shah enjoys credibility within India's startup ecosystem, maintains strong relationships across industry and policy circles, and understands the nuances of the market in a way few Silicon Valley executives do. While regulatory challenges are unlikely to disappear, Meta now has a leader capable of speaking both the language of Menlo Park and that of New Delhi.The bet appears straightforward for Zuckerberg and Meta. Advertising built the company, but messaging, AI and commerce are expected to define its next chapter. And if that chapter is written with India at its centre, Shah may be the executive best placed to tell the story.WhatsApp's biggest challenge is no longer acquiring users. It already has them. The challenge is turning that unprecedented scale into a sustainable business without losing the simplicity that made the app indispensable in the first place.That balancing act now belongs to Kunal Shah.
Article Summary: Kunal Shah is expected to step down from his role as Chief Executive Officer of CRED and join Meta's global leadership team. Shah takes over at a pivotal moment for WhatsApp. Nearly 12 years after Meta paid $19 billion for the messaging app, the company is still figuring out how to turn its scale into a business.

Article Title: Australia doubles fines for tech giants failing to block kids from social media
Article Text:
Five major platforms under investigation



Meta's Instagram and Facebook

Google's YouTube

Snap's Snapchat

TikTok

5 million accounts blocked



The Australian government announced on Saturday (June 27) that it will double the maximum financial penalties for tech companies failing to enforce the social media ban for kids under 16, following a report which claimed that teenagers are easily bypassing the restrictions. Under the newly proposed changes, the maximum fine for a social media platform’s systemic failure to block children under the age of 16 will jump from A$49.5 million to a staggering A$99 million ($68 million USD), according to a report by news agency Reuters.In addition to the massive fines, the government plans to strengthen the information-gathering powers of its internet regulator, the eSafety Commissioner. This will legally force social media giants to turn over explicit internal evidence detailing exactly what steps they are taking to prevent underage users from creating accounts.A spokesperson for the Prime Minister stated that the exact timeline for introducing these stricter amendments to Parliament has not been finalised.The regulatory crackdown comes as the government confirmed that the eSafety Commission is actively investigating five of the world's largest social media platforms for potential non-compliance:Australia’s six-month-old minimum age law is being closely watched by international governments eager to tackle youth mental and physical health crises.The UK, for instance, recently announced even stricter upcoming restrictions that will expand to target gaming and live-streaming apps.“I'm heartened by the shift in conversation and the global momentum we’ve seen since introducing the social media minimum age, but it’s clear Big Tech are not doing enough to comply with the law — there are still too many children on social media,” Prime Minister Anthony Albanese said in an official statement.The government defended the overall effectiveness of the ban, noting that tech companies have deactivated or restricted more than 5 million underage accounts since the legislation went live. However, a report suggests that teenagers are easily adapting, detailing that 85% of Australians aged 12 to 15 were still actively using social media three months after the ban took effect.Numerous studies highlight that current age-verification tools, like asking a user to upload a selfie, are easy for kids to trick. Even worse, researchers found that in many instances, tech platforms never even prompted the children to verify their age in the first place.
Article Summary: The maximum fine for a social media platform’s systemic failure to block children under the age of 16 will jump from A$49.5 million to a staggering A$99 million. In addition to the massive fines, the government plans to strengthen the information-gathering powers of its internet regulator.

Article Title: Justice department takes down 400 illegal World Cup streaming sites in global piracy raid
Article Text:
Global piracy network interrupted



High viewership fuels piracy demand



In a massive crackdown on digital piracy, the US government says that it has seized nearly 400 internet domains that were being used to illegally stream live World Cup matches. The officials announced that the federal operation was designed to disrupt international criminal networks profiting from the global popularity of the tournament.According to a report by news agency Reuters, the US Department of Justice (DOJ) revealed that the illegal streaming sites were identified and targeted with the direct assistance of football’s international governing body, FIFA, alongside major media conglomerates, including NBCUniversal and Warner Bros. Discovery.According to federal prosecutors, the seized domains were engineered to broadcast copyright-protected, real-time streams of World Cup matches exactly as they were being played and aired by official broadcasting partners.The illicit operations stretched beyond US borders as the DOJ confirmed that servers and domains tied to the unauthorised broadcasts were physically targeted and taken down in Peru and Bulgaria. Additional coordinated digital disruptions took place across Europe and South America, specifically hitting illegal operations in Croatia, Romania, Poland and Colombia.Federal authorities warned that these pirated streams pose severe security risks to the everyday sports fans who click on them.“These streamers not only violate copyright laws but also expose viewers to potential threats, including malware attacks and unsecure connections that can compromise personal and financial data,” said Eric Weindorf, a special agent in charge at Homeland Security Investigations (HSI).The federal crackdown comes as the World Cup enjoys global attention. Co-hosted by the US, Canada and Mexico, the tournament kicked off on June 11, with matches scheduled across 16 host cities through July 19. According to data released by FIFA, stadium attendance for this tournament has already set an all-time record.
Article Summary: The US government has seized nearly 400 internet domains that were being used to illegally stream live World Cup matches. The federal operation was designed to disrupt international criminal networks profiting from the global popularity of the tournament. Federal authorities warned that these pirated streams pose severe security risks to the everyday sports fans who click on them.

Article Title: In 2017, Google researchers introduced the transformer, laying the groundwork for AI chatbots
Article Text:
On the monitor, a chatbot interface is visible with the text 'Humans First' displayed prominently. Image Credit: Wikimedia Commons

Why Attention Outperformed Earlier Approaches

The basics of today's chatbot technology

Illustrations for the Transformer, and attention mechanism. Image Credit: Wikimedia Commons

From a simple translation tool to infrastructure

Why the blueprint is relevant now

In 2017, Google released a scientific paper, which spoke of an algorithm that led to the development of what is now called the transformer architecture. More than nine years later, this architectural change has significantly influenced how machines generate and process language. It became a key step in bridging the gap between hard-coded computer programs and modern AI chatbots.The algorithms for processing languages before this discovery required step-by-step processing of the information. When a machine processed a sentence, it had to handle the words one by one, from the first to the last. As a result, it was difficult for machines to grasp the meaning of an entire paragraph because they had to finish processing earlier words first.With transformers, attention-based processing became possible. This allowed the network to consider the whole sentence at once and understand how words relate to one another.It was an architectural innovation, and its influence on technology was enormous. According to a 2024 study indexed in PubMed , the most modern systems, such as BERT and GPT, are built completely on this architecture.According to the research, the 2017 design remains widely used. Instead, the most advanced systems currently being developed are built directly on this design. Its versatility helped AI software move from translating individual sentences to handling more complex dialogues.The original Google paper was designed to perform a specific task: language translation. Researchers needed just a way to make translation faster and more accurate. However, the design turned out to have much greater potential than many in the industry initially expected.When researchers showed how well transformers handled language, they opened the door to many other applications. People realized that the same approach could be used not only for translating languages but also for generating essays, answering customer service queries, and summarizing texts.The speed at which the technology spread across sectors suggests its practicality. Rather than remaining confined to tech labs, it became a go-to design for AI applications in healthcare, programming, and art.Modern AI chatbots differ from earlier versions because, instead of matching keywords to preset answers, they use transformers as the basis of their operation.Despite rapid advances in artificial intelligence, the 2017 blueprint remains highly influential. The results of a scientific review indexed in the PubMed database in 2026 and dedicated to advanced language systems have proved that the big language models are still based on the mentioned transformer architecture. This suggests the innovation is not a temporary trend that disappears when new ideas emerge.It is rare for a single research paper to have such a broad influence. The current success of chatbots reflects years of product development. It reflects years of step-by-step work built on the structure created almost ten years ago. By abandoning sequential processing and adopting attention, Google developers helped create a new way for people and computers to interact.
Article Summary: Google released a scientific paper in 2009 that led to the development of what is now called the transformer architecture. It became a key step in bridging the gap between hard-coded computer programs and modern AI chatbots. Its versatility helped AI software move from translating individual sentences to handling more complex dialogues. Despite rapid advances in artificial intelligence, the 2017 blueprint remains highly influential.

Article Title: PayU launches AI-ready developer tools for faster payment integrations

Create and send payment links

Generate reports

The tools aim to simplify integration and operations for developers and merchants by bringing payment infrastructure directly into their everyday coding environments.The PayU CLI is designed for developers and operations teams managing daily payment workflows. Instead of navigating complex dashboards, teams can now:The CLI supports multi-account management and seamless switching between test and production environments, making it particularly useful for DevOps teams handling multiple merchants.Together, Builder MCP and PayU CLI cover the full payment lifecycle. Developers can use Builder MCP during implementation to ship integrations faster, while operations teams can rely on PayU CLI for ongoing management of transactions, settlements, and refunds directly from the terminal.Narendra Babu, CTO of PayU, said: “AI has fundamentally transformed software development. Merchants and their tech teams are now looking for lightning-fast integrations and operational flexibility. Our new CLI and Builder MCP tools solve for each of these requirements by bringing PayU’s payment infrastructure to where developers work — in the terminal.”A developer using Cursor can request Builder MCP for PayU checkout integration code and receive ready-to-deploy JavaScript with error handling, reducing integration time from days to hours. Meanwhile, operations teams can use PayU CLI to instantly execute commands such as “create an INR 5,000 payment link” or “get yesterday’s settlement report” without dashboard navigation
Article Summary: PayU, India’s leading diversified fintech platform, has announced two new developer tools designed to streamline modern payment workflows. Article Title: In 1996, Dolby Digital helped bring surround sound to DVDs and home theaters
Article Text:
Dolby Digital, originally synonymous with Dolby AC-3 is the name for a family of audio compression technologies developed by Dolby Laboratories. Called Dolby Stereo Digital until 1995, it uses lossy compression (except for Dolby TrueHD). Image Credit: Wikipedia

Engineering tricks, not data-heavy ones

Using human biology in the design

A DVD player is a machine that plays DVDs produced under both the DVD-Video and DVD-Audio technical standards, two different and incompatible standards. Image Credit: Wikipedia

From laboratory tests to living rooms at home

Remember the mid-90s? Going to the movies meant being enveloped in surround sound, experiencing booming explosion sound, and listening to the clear dialogue from the speakers.Around 1996, Dolby engineers improved AC-3, also known as Dolby Digital , helping make surround sound practical for home theater and DVD systems.The sentence is missing a period and reads unfinished. The traditional approach was to store each speaker channel in uncompressed form. The result was that it used too much bandwidth. Dolby therefore developed perceptual coding, which preserves the parts of a signal people are most likely to hear while discarding less noticeable detail.According to a review of research conducted on the development of immersive music experience in PubMed , contemporary immersive music experiences are a culmination of a very long history of technological development that has gone from mono to stereo and eventually to the current surround systems of sound playback. Dolby AC-3 followed the same broader shift toward more immersive audio playback.To achieve that efficiency, the AC-3 coder used psychoacoustics, the science of human sound perception.Humans are able to perceive sound extremely well, but still face some natural limitations. For example, if two sounds occur at the same time, the louder one can mask the quieter one, making it harder to hear.Dolby researchers used the ear's masking behavior to compress audio efficiently. The AC-3 system analyzed the soundtrack to identify the sounds listeners were most likely to hear and preserved those more accurately while using less data for less noticeable details.The format had an advantage because it was designed around human hearing. Processing five audio channels together instead of separately helped distribute sound more smoothly across the listening space.Users did not need to understand filter banks, overlapping windows, or algorithms to benefit from the system. All they needed to do was play the disc.The 1996 improvements set a new baseline for home cinema expectations. People no longer had to settle for a simple stereo mix. Now, dialogue remained firmly rooted in the center, ambient noise like rain and wind came from the side speakers, and moving sound effects traveled smoothly across the space.In this way, the technology helped shape the more immersive emotional experience of home movies. It demonstrated how the greatest consumer standards do not triumph through power alone but rather through meeting the challenge of difficult engineering constraints in an emotionally compelling way.
Article Summary: Around 1996, Dolby engineers improved AC-3, also known as Dolby Digital, helping make surround sound practical for home theater and DVD systems. Dolby researchers used the ear's masking behavior to compress audio efficiently. Processing five audio channels together instead of separately helped distribute sound more smoothly across the listening space.

Article Title: In 1989, Phil Katz introduced the ZIP format, making large computer files easier to store and share
Article Text:
Phillip Walter Katz (November 3, 1962 – April 14, 2000) was a computer programmer best known as the co-creator of the ZIP file format for data compression, and the author of PKZIP, a program for creating zip files that ran under DOS. Image Credit: Wikipedia

A simple format with big goals

Phillip Walter Katz (November 3, 1962 – April 14, 2000) was a computer programmer best known as the co-creator of the ZIP file format for data compression, and the author of PKZIP, a program for creating zip files that ran under DOS. Image Credit: Wikipedia

The problem that disappeared

From single files to file packaging

Back in the late 1980s, using a computer required a lot of patience. In a time before cloud storage, fast internet, and multi-terabyte hard drives, transferring an electronic file could be time-consuming and cumbersome. To share a bulky document or software application, it might have been necessary to split the file into several floppy disks or to use excruciatingly slow dial-up telephone lines.The situation changed in 1989 thanks to Phil Katz, a software developer who created the ZIP file format. This amazing technology helped to make digital files smaller, more convenient to store, and easy to transfer between various computer systems. An idea that at the beginning seemed to be trivial turned out to be an integral element of modern computer infrastructure.It all started with Phil Katz and his company, PKWARE. According to the information from the Library of Congress , the ZIP format was initially created by Katz in 1989. It is described there as a portable archive format designed for cross-platform data interchange and efficient storage.ZIP was simple rather than sophisticated. It was designed to solve a mundane but frustrating problem: how to make files easier to move and reuse on computer systems with far less storage and speed than today's machines.ZIP emerged in the optimal time frame. In 1989, computer users had to carefully consider every megabyte and floppy disk. The format could compress data and pack several files into one, offering some breathing space. Although it began as a simple utility, it quickly became more than that.It did not take long for ZIP to become a widespread tool. The format eventually became a de facto standard and evolved throughout the years to include much larger file sizes and advanced encryption capabilities. Many utilities from that era became obsolete as hardware changed; ZIP, by contrast, evolved to meet users' changing needs.Another reason for the format's spread was Katz's decision to release it openly. According to the format notes, Katz released the .ZIP extension and the file-format specification into the public domain.Thus, by eliminating legal complications and licensing restrictions, Katz made it easy for software developers to create compatible tools. Inability to read and produce the same file type would render it much less useful. Therefore, the success of the ZIP format is a technical feat as well as a social one. It compresses information and helps different software applications work together.Indeed, one can hardly notice the cleverness of ZIP anymore, since it has become such a common tool. NIST defines the term "compressed file" in regard to the reduction of the burden of storing and transmitting data with the help of its CSRC Glossary . Old utilities such as PKZIP and WinZip are still used as standard examples of this technology.This was the core problem that ZIP solved. It takes time and effort to transfer large files. In the late 1980s, compression could mean the difference between a usable file and one that was too large to handle.Such a utility revolutionized the relationship between users and technology. Transferring files, backing up your PC, downloading applications, or transferring bunches of documents became easy tasks. Users no longer had to understand the mathematical algorithms behind compression; they just wanted their files transferred quickly and intact.Over time, the role of the format experienced a tremendous change. The format became the basis for several file formats and packaging layers, including Microsoft’s OOXML (.docx), OpenDocument formats, EPUB books, and Java JAR files.Those storage techniques now work behind the scenes in digital documents and ebooks. What began as a desktop utility has become an invisible but essential part of digital workflows worldwide.Nowadays, Phil Katz’s 1989 invention is a strong example of a practical technical solution that has stood the test of time. It began as a solution to the limits of early personal computers and evolved as digital technology advanced.
Article Summary: Phillip Walter Katz (November 3, 1962 – April 14, 2000) was a computer programmer best known as the co-creator of the ZIP file format for data compression. He was also the author of PKZIP, a program for creating zip files that ran under DOS.

Article Title: In 1979, Sony and Philips formalized the compact disc standard, turning digital audio into a home format
Article Text:
Compact Disc preserving music, memories, and digital media. Image Credit: Wikimedia Commons

The partnership was built on complementary strengths

Taking the digital sound out of the lab

The Sony CDP-101 was the world's first commercially released compact disc player. Image Credit: Wikipedia

A rulebook that brought the trust of consumers

A long-lasting legacy outside the world of music

During the last decade of the 20th century, the experience of enjoying music at home was both highly personalized and technically imperfect. Vinyl records could be warped, skipped, or dusty, whereas cassettes could hiss, tangle, and stretch during use. People were ready to forgive the technical imperfections for the chance to enjoy the sound of their favourite artists in their homes. In reality, a technological revolution was taking shape against the backdrop of all that. A corporate deal in 1979 marked an important turning point in home listening.Sony, one of the largest Japanese electronics corporations, and Philips, one of the biggest Dutch companies, did not try to wage a tough battle for domination. Instead, they chose to cooperate and paved the road for developing the compact disc. The disc became an opportunity for moving digital audio out from highly specialized research laboratories and right into everyday home stereos. What is more, the partnership was not only about inventing another piece of hardware. It was a story of cooperation between the most powerful rivals of those times, having opposite engineering approaches.Before meeting, both companies faced the same issue but approached it from different directions.These differences made the partnership highly practical. They did not have to start from scratch; instead, they could combine both research paths into a single consumer product. As described by an educational source from the University of California, San Diego, while the CD was initially developed by Philips, its standards have been jointly set by Sony to provide for only one consumer product on the market. Thus, by combining the optical technology developed by Philips and the digital technology by Sony, it became possible to manufacture this product in huge volumes and not confuse consumers with several products.The partnership aimed not only to perfect the technology but also to bring it to millions of users. They understood that if the technology remained too complicated or too costly, it would stay in the laboratory. According to one of the indexed reviews about standards in PubMed , the standards for the compact disc were created with the retail market specifically in mind. The developers had a very clear goal to get the regular customers interested in buying the players and the discs themselves.Their attention to the consumer needs greatly contributed to the unprecedented speed of development of the format. Digital audio, which was initially a quite general and futuristic idea, turned into a household product that could coexist peacefully with the traditional amplifier. In particular, a historical exhibit of East Carolina University states that right from its release in 1982, the compact disc started replacing vinyl records and cassette tapes in use because of being a much more effective storage medium for music.To make sure that this new format would succeed around the world, the engineering team had to develop an exact set of rules for every producer to follow. This task was accomplished in the early 1980s when the joint technical specifications were documented in a book that came to be known all over the world as the Red Book. As the University of Maryland engineering case study reveals in detail, the Red Book became the official standards document, which laid out the rules for the arrangement, decoding, and playback of audio data on the compact disc.The Red Book helped turn the compact disc into a consumer standard. Thanks to the common rulebook, a CD purchased in London would work perfectly in a device produced in Tokyo or Amsterdam. This gave manufacturers a common standard and reassured consumers that their purchase would remain compatible. The standard helped ensure high sound quality and gave consumers confidence in compatibility.The effects of the 1979 partnership extended far beyond the music industry. With time, scientists came to understand that if a medium could provide flawless audio quality, then that medium could store vast amounts of computer data as well. Later retrospectives note that the compact disc’s design helped shape the development of the CD-ROM and influenced personal computers, software, and video games in later decades."History of Music Reproduction" by the University of Southern California reveals that Sony and Philips designed the compact disc in order to revolutionize the world of digital audio. Making their design consumer-oriented, they made an attempt to connect two worlds – the old analog one and the emerging digital world. The compact disc showed how a well-designed technical standard can influence culture far beyond its original purpose.
Article Summary: In 1979, Sony and Philips teamed up to develop the first commercially released compact disc player. The Red Book became the official standards document, which laid out the rules for the arrangement, decoding, and playback of audio data on the compact disc. The compact disc’s design helped shape the development of the CD-ROM and influenced personal computers, software, and video games.

Article Title: Perplexity CEO Aravind Srinivas: One founder habit that I had to learn and then unlearn is…
Article Text:
Aravind Srinivas, Perplexity co-founder and CEO, on the founder habit he had to learn and then unlearn.

Perplexity CEO shares the 'Beautiful Mind' trap that fools smart founders

Why Aravind Srinivas says shipping fast beats perfecting the plan

Aravind Srinivas spent years in research labs learning to prize the big idea worked out carefully on paper. Then he built Perplexity, the AI search engine now reportedly valued at around 20 billion dollars, and discovered that the habit he had trained hardest was the one he needed to drop. The Perplexity co-founder and CEO says his biggest founder lesson was unlearning the belief that thinking always precedes building. His new rule is blunter—action produces information.Srinivas describes the trap with some specificity. People want to come up with a grand idea on a whiteboard, run it once, and watch everything click into place. He blames the movies for this. You write the paper because that is how invention looks in "A Beautiful Mind" or the Stephen Hawking biopic—a flash of insight scrawled across glass, then the problem is solved. Real work, he says, is far less cinematic.The pull toward planning is really a pull toward safety. A plan feels controllable. It can be polished endlessly without ever meeting a single user. But a plan does not generate information. A live experiment does. Many companies, Srinivas notes, do exactly the wrong thing—they keep refining the document because the document feels safer than contact with reality.His replacement habit is simple to state and harder to practice: small-scale experiments, repeated fast.Ship something. Put it in real hands. Watch what people actually do, not what they say they will do. Update the hypothesis and run it again. That iterative loop, not the whiteboard, is what shaped Perplexity in its early days.The principle travels well beyond AI. Consider wildfire insurance, where property owners make the same error in another vocabulary. They do the work—clear defensible space, harden buildings, improve roofs, vents and access—and then assume the market will reward the effort. It usually will not. Insurance does not pay out for effort. It pays for proof.That is Srinivas's underlying point. Effort that stays hidden inside a plan changes nothing. Effort exposed to reality, fast and often, is the only kind that teaches you anything.
Article Summary: Aravind Srinivas, co-founder and CEO of Perplexity, talks about the 'Beautiful Mind' trap that fools smart founders. He says his biggest founder lesson was unlearning the belief that thinking always precedes building. His replacement habit is simple to state and harder to practice: small-scale experiments, repeated fast.

Article Title: Land donated for a park for $10 30 years ago, sold for $10 million to build a data center; US city’s Community services head says he doesn't need approval from any resident because…
Article Text:
Representational image

Why are city officials saying that the project does not need approval

Residents file appeal against data centre citing original deed

A Texas city is facing a legal challenge and public criticism following its decision to dispose of land intended to be used for the creation of a public park by selling the same to create a data center facility. The 87-acre land in question was donated to the city more than 30 years ago for $10, on the condition that it would be utilised as a park. However, in 2025, the city sold the same land for $10 million to a data centre construction company.According to a report by 404 Media, the land was donated by the Bland family in 1999 to the Texas Parks and Recreation Foundation, with the deed stating it was to be held in trust for future use as parkland by Williamson County. Over the following years, ownership changed hands several times before the Taylor Economic Development Corporation (TEDC) sold the property to Blueprint, a data center developer, for $10 million. The site is now planned to house a 135,000-square-foot data center.Residents opposing the project questioned why city officials did not stop the development. However, Daniel Seguin, Taylor's executive director of community services, said Blueprint did not require additional city approval because the site's existing zoning already permits data centers.“Blueprint Projects did not require City approval to use the property as a data center because the property’s existing Employment Center zoning already allowed such use,” Seguin told 404 Media.He added, “The City of Taylor’s Land Development Code primarily regulates form, not function. The only approvals that our code requires are for the general layout of the buildings, landscaping, impervious cover, etc. The developer has not advanced the project with the City beyond the Employment Center Plan. To break ground, the developer would still have to secure the City’s approval for platting and building permits. This process has not yet been initiated.”The city's website also states that it cannot simply reject data center projects. According to its FAQ section, "Can the City just say no to data centers? In short, no."Pamela Griffin, whose family has lived near the property for generations, said local activists uncovered the original 1999 deed after she recalled that the farmer had promised her father the land would become a park for local children.According to court records and property documents, the deed required the land to be used as parkland. Griffin and several family members hired a lawyer and filed a lawsuit challenging the development.Griffin said, “We gotta get this park back for this community that should have been built a long time ago.”The lawsuit was dismissed by a trial court, and a request to stop construction while the appeal proceeds was also denied. Griffin's lawyer later told her, “Pam, if you’d been fighting an apartment complex or anything else […] you would have won that case.” The plaintiffs have appealed the ruling.Residents have also raised concerns about possible impacts on noise, electricity, water use and nearby property values. Griffin said, “We can’t afford it. I got a lot of old people in our community that can’t afford to move.”The city says the project includes measures such as noise barriers and a closed-loop cooling system to reduce its impact. Officials also estimate the facility will generate about $30 million in tax revenue over the next decade, including roughly $20 million for the local school district.Seguin said, “Data centers create a significant net financial benefit for cities because they generate a lot of new tax revenue without also increasing demand on city services and infrastructure. Because they don’t employ many people or attract many visitors, the facilities don’t increase traffic or emergency service calls. They also don’t increase the need for housing and classrooms.”Griffin said the legal challenge is about preserving the original purpose of the land, adding, “My family didn’t hire the lawyer to sue the company to get money. We’re suing for the deed to build a park for this community.”
Article Summary: The 87-acre land in question was donated to the city more than 30 years ago for $10, on the condition that it would be utilised as a park. However, in 2025, the city sold the same land to a data centre construction company. A lawsuit was dismissed by a trial court, and a request to stop construction while the appeal proceeds was also denied.
